The techniques of handling a liquid fuel which is a solid at room temperature and which must be preheated to about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ before any transfer can take place must be determined experimentally.

The following systems must be developed for such a fuel circuit: (1) evacuation, (2) flushing, (3) loading, (4) preheating, (5) transferring, (6) cleaning, (7) pressurizing, (8) monitoring, (9) purifying, (10) storing, and (11) instrumenting.

The general requirement is for the experimental determination of techniques of handling a liquid coolant which, in the case of sodium, would be solid at room temperature and would require preheating to at least $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ before any transfer would take place.
If the coolant was sodium-potassium eutectic alloy, which is liquid at room temperatures, it would not require preheating but would require pressurizing at a temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A pump will be required which will handle about 150 to 200 gpm; for the ARE application it is anticipated that commercially available pumps can be modified for this purpose.

The techniques of cleaning the cooling system of the reactor and transferring coolant to it in a pure state and of providing equipment for maintaining purity of the coolant require considerable development in order to assure satisfactory handling at a maximum coolant temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
It is anticipated that in the course of the experimentation a heat source comparable to the output of the ARE (1 to 3 megawatts) will be a necessity for pretesting components of the system.

The uranium metal is being fabricated into disks approximately 3 in. in diameter and 0.010 in, thick by first rolling billets to the required thickness and then punching the disks.

The first batch of 1200 disks delivered were found to be heavily coated with black oxide, probably formed during hot rolling, which, because of the nonadherence of the oxide, presented a serious contamination and accountability problem.
The oxide was readily removed by leaching in concentrated nitric acid, after which the metal remained at room temperature in an atmosphere of about $15\%$ relative humidity.
It has been necessary, however, to lower the target weight of all disks by about $5\%$ because of the weight loss in the first batch during leaching.

The 5-Mev Van de Graaff accelerator which has been purchased by the United States Air Force for use in the joint NEPA-ORNL accelerator laboratory successfully passed its acceptance tests at the High Voltage Engineering Corporation, Cambridge, Mass., on December 28, 1950.

During the course of the tests, the accelerator produced a magnetically analyzed proton beam of from 6 to $7\mu \mathrm{a}$ in its optimum operating range of from 2.5 to 4 Mev and operated successfully at somewhat lower beam currents at energy extremes of 0.5 and 5.4 Mev.
The fact that no such accelerator has yet yielded nuclear data above 4.25 Mev lends substantiation to the claim that this is the finest electrostatic accelerator in the world.
During January and February the accelerator was disassembled, painted, crated, and shipped to Oak Ridge, where its reassembly is now in progress in Building 9201-2 in the Y-12 Area.

The last two ANP quarterly progress reports (ORNL-858 and 919) have discussed some of the characteristics of liquid-fuel systems, and have described the preliminary phases of the research designed to produce satisfactory liquids.

At this time it appears that the ternary system sodium fluoride-beryllium fluoride-uranium fluoride will find use in the ANP and ARE reactors, since this fuel combines suitable uranium density (about $80\mathrm{lb / cu ft}$ ) in a fuel solution with a satisfactory (below $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) melting point.

This system was previously studied by Kraus.(1) Cooling curves on the lower-eutectic-point mixture, 26 mole % $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and 74 mole % NaF, and on a mixture of one other composition, $18\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and $82\%$ NaF, were determined at the beginning of this work, and the results obtained seemed to confirm the published data.

However, in the course of the investigation of the ternary system NaF-KF- $\mathrm{UF_4}$ , it began to appear that Kraus' equilibrium diagram might be in error, particularly in the regions above 50 mole % $\mathrm{UF_4}$ .
In addition, Zachariasen's study of this system by the X-ray diffraction method(2) showed the existence of several compounds in addition to the $\mathrm{NaUF_5}$ compound that Kraus' data indicated.
In the range 0 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ the equilibrium curve is in essential agreement with Kraus' data.
The melting point of the eutectic at 26 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ is $615 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

There appears to be good evidence for the existence of the $\mathrm{Na_2UF_6}$ compound although the thermal data in the 30 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ range hardly permit unequivocal interpretation.
Zachariasen showed that $\mathrm{Na_2UF_6}$ exists in two modifications, and it appears likely that the low breaks in the 0 to 25 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ range are also due to solid transition.
The higher eutectic point is at about 56 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ instead of the previously accepted 67 mole%.
The melting point of this eutectic is $680 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The thermal data do not show the existence of the $\mathrm{Na_3UF_7}$ compound identified by Zachariasen in the 25 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ region.

Study of this system was begun early in the present research program but was discontinued after the existence of the high-melting compound $\mathrm{K}_3\mathrm{UF}_7$ was found and the absence of any low-melting eutectic in the 0 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF}_4$ range was established.

The thermal data appear to confirm the existence of about all the compounds that Zachariasen found in this system, (3) but it must be admitted that the evidence for some of the compounds is not very strong.
The eutectics in this system are all in the 730 to $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ range; this variation is only slightly greater than the experimental error.
The two compounds having congruent melting points, $\mathrm{K}_3\mathrm{UF}_7$ and $\mathrm{KUF}_5$ , melt at $970 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $780 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively.
Figure 6.3 shows the isotherms and the known eutectics in this system.
The lowest eutectic believed to exist is that at 27 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , 43.5 mole $\%$ NaF, and 29.5 mole $\%$ KF, having a melting point of $530 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, a well-defined break in the cooling curves for several points near the above-mentioned composition appeared at about $430^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
None of these mixtures appeared to be liquid below the $530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ eutectic.

Two compounds, $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{KUF}_7$ and $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{KUF}_8$ , are possible in the area where the $430^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ break appears, and it is possible that a solid reaction occurs with the formation of a stable compound.
There is a fairly large area in the diagram, in the range 24 to 33 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , with a maximum melting point of $570 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
There appears to be a eutectic at approximately 55 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , 30 mole $\%$ NaF, and 15 mole $\%$ KF with a melting point of $670 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Another eutectic at $620 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ apparently exists in the lower right-hand corner of the diagram, the exact location of which was not determined (see Filtration Experiments, below).
